title: Welcome to My Analytics Lab
text:
Hello! My name is Oznur Meydan.
I am a senior Industrial Engineering student at Hacettepe University interested in data analytics, optimization, and machine learning.



 Back to top

title: About Me
text:
Long-Term Internship
Operations and Business Development Intern – Enerjisa Eşarj, Ankara, Turkey
2025 – Present

Support data-driven decision-making processes through market analysis, performance monitoring, and strategic evaluations related to electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
Contribute to analytical reports focusing on network growth, operational performance, and strategic partnerships.
Assist in evaluating charging station performance and market dynamics within the EV ecosystem.

title: About Me
text:
Internships
Production and Sales Intern – Eltaş Transformer Industry and Trade Inc., İzmir, Turkey
2023

Completed a two-month rotational internship in production and sales departments.
Gained hands-on experience in transformer manufacturing processes, workflow efficiency, and quality control practices.
Assisted the sales team with quotation preparation and tracking customer inquiries.

Research Support Intern – Magiçe Design & Technology, Ankara, Turkey
2023

Supported research and analytical tasks in design and technology-focused projects.
Assisted in data collection, reporting, and project development processes.

title: Assignment 1
text: My first assignment has two parts.

title: Assignment 1
text:
(a) Video Summary
I watched the talk “Veri Bilimi ve Endüstri Mühendisliği Üzerine Sohbetler - Baykal Hafızoğlu & Erdi Daşdemir.”
In this talk, Baykal Hafızoğlu talks about his education and career journey. He graduated from Industrial Engineering at METU, completed his master’s degree, and then moved to the United States for his PhD at Arizona State University. After finishing his PhD, he started working in industry and continued his career in operations research, optimization, and analytics roles in different companies in the US.
One important point in the talk is that industrial engineering provides a strong background for analytics. He explains that the courses taken in industrial engineering, such as statistics, optimization, simulation, and data-related courses, are connected to descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, and prescriptive analytics. He also says that students should understand how these areas are related instead of seeing courses as separate subjects.
Another important message is that solving a model is not enough by itself. He emphasizes that analytical models should be delivered to end users in a usable way. According to him, deployment, user interface, early prototyping, and feedback from users are very important in real projects. He says that even a strong analytical model may fail if it cannot be explained well or used easily by people.
Overall, the talk shows that technical skills such as mathematical modeling, coding, and optimization are important, but communication, problem definition, and delivering practical solutions are also necessary for success in this field.

Open-Ended Question
Question:
According to Baykal Hafızoğlu, why is deployment as important as building a strong analytical or optimization model?
Answer:
Because a model creates value only when end users can actually use it in practice. In the talk, he explains that many projects fail not because the model is weak, but because the solution is not delivered properly. Deployment, user interface, early prototypes, and user feedback are important because they help transform a technical model into a practical tool that people can use in real business settings.


Multiple Choice Question
Question:
Which of the following skills did Baykal Hafızoğlu especially recommend for students who want to work in optimization and operations research?

Only memorizing solver commands

Mathematical modeling and coding skills

Learning only visualization tools

Focusing only on academic writing

Correct Answer:
B) Mathematical modeling and coding skills

title: Assignment 1
text:
(b) Data Analysis with R

Import the dataset

library(dslabs)
data("polls_us_election_2016")

data <- polls_us_election_2016



Define personal variables

my_first <- "Oznur"
my_birth_year <- 2000



Calculate k

k <- (nchar(my_first) + my_birth_year) %% 15 + 8
k

[1] 18




Display rows depending on k

if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  selected_rows <- head(data, k)
} else {
  selected_rows <- tail(data, k)
}

head(na_per_column, 8)

rawpoll_mcmullin adjpoll_mcmullin  rawpoll_johnson  adjpoll_johnson 
            4178             4178             1409             1409 
           grade       samplesize            state        startdate 
             429                1                0                0 




Replace NA values

new_data <- data

numeric_cols <- sapply(new_data, is.numeric)

for (col in names(new_data)[numeric_cols]) {
  new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- my_birth_year + k
}


char_factor_cols <- sapply(new_data, function(x) is.character(x) | is.factor(x))

for (col in names(new_data)[char_factor_cols]) {
  new_data[[col]] <- as.character(new_data[[col]])
  new_data[[col]][is.na(new_data[[col]])] <- paste0(my_first, "_", k)
}



Display rows from new dataset

if (k %% 2 == 0) {
  new_selected_rows <- head(new_data, k)
} else {
  new_selected_rows <- tail(new_data, k)
}
